food_name: Nga Thone Pya Paung (Burmese Steamed Lemon Fish with Rice)
aliases: ငါးသံပုရာပေါင်း
Burmese steamed lemon fish with rice

english: Burmese steamed lemon fish with rice
burmese: ငါးသံပုရာပေါင်း

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Burmese nga thone pya paung. FOODON_03315595 (steamed fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Fish, steamed or boiled) is the closest parent. No specific Burmese steamed lemon fish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Burmese dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue regional Southeast Asian prepared fish dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of steamed lemon fish (approximately 150 g) served over steamed white rice (approximately 250 g), as typically plated at a Myanmar restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia (2nd edition) — steamed freshwater fish and cooked white rice components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for nga thone pya paung exists in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 37.5% steamed freshwater fish (nga thone pya, analogous to snakehead fish or similar lean freshwater fish per FAO/INFOODS SEA) and 62.5% cooked white rice (per FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy item 20045). Seasonings (lemongrass, ginger, garlic, turmeric, lime juice) contribute negligible macronutrients at typical quantities. Values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cooked white rice (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nga thone pya paung. The GI of 64 is derived from the GI of cooked white rice (GI ~64, medium, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008), which constitutes approximately 62.5% of the dish by weight. The fish and seasoning components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 64 × carbohydrate per serving [82 g from 400 g dish × 0.205]) / 100 = approximately 52.5 g GL. This is a composite analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salted fish preparation and added salt
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 36–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base gives this dish a medium GI of approximately 64; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size, consider substituting brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with low saturated fat; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The steamed lean fish component provides heart-healthy protein with low saturated fat (approximately 0.3 g per 100 g), which is favourable for cardiovascular health, though the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nga Thone Pya Paung (ငါးသံပုရာပေါင်း) is a traditional Burmese dish consisting of steamed lemon fish — locally known as nga thone pya — served over or alongside steamed white rice. The fish is typically marinated or seasoned with aromatics such as lemongrass, ginger, garlic, turmeric, and lime or lemon juice before being gently steamed, resulting in a delicate, moist texture with a mildly fragrant, lightly sour flavour profile. The dish is a staple of Myanmar home cooking and is also served in Myanmar restaurants in Singapore, such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie dish (approximately 128 kcal per 100 g) providing a meaningful source of lean protein from the fish (approximately 8.5 g per 100 g) and carbohydrate energy from the rice (approximately 20.5 g per 100 g). The fat content is low (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g), with a favourable unsaturated fat profile. The dish has a medium glycemic index (approximately 64) driven primarily by the white rice component, making portion awareness relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate and should be considered by those with hypertension. The steaming preparation method preserves nutrients and avoids added oils, making this a relatively healthful choice within the Burmese culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for nga thone pya paung was found in any approved primary source (FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of steamed freshwater fish and cooked white rice components using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data. GI is analogously estimated from white rice GI per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Fish and seafood' as the dish is primarily a steamed fish preparation served with rice; the fish is the defining component. 2. Cuisine tags 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' in the input appear to be erroneous field survey platform tags and have been disregarded; this dish does not contain noodles. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this food type and the composite estimation does not yield a reliable value. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this food type. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this food type. 6. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero as steamed freshwater fish and plain cooked rice contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; this is naturally zero for this preparation. 7. All -1 sentinel values reflect genuine absence of data in the approved source composite and are documented per G3 requirements. 8. Price data (SGD 25.00) preserved from input for reference only; not used in nutritional assessment. 9. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); mandatory multilingual coverage for Burmese cuisine is not specified in the scoring model's enumerated list, so local_names fields for those languages are null. The Burmese script name (ငါးသံပုရာပေါင်း) is captured in aliases_by_language.burmese and the flat aliases list. 10. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (64 × 82 g available carbohydrate from 400 g serving) / 100 = 52.5.
